blocks.title: A — Opening
blocks.outcome: Students understand what the Tour is, feel welcomed, and see AI used once before any teaching begins.

blocks.title: B — AI/LLM Mental Model
blocks.outcome: Students can explain context window, tokens, and hallucination in their own words.

blocks.title: C — Vibe Coding Mindset
blocks.outcome: Students can articulate the difference between manual coding and vibe coding, and when each is appropriate.

blocks.title: D — Live Demo: Claude Desktop vs Claude Code
blocks.outcome: Students have seen both surfaces and understand when each fits.

blocks.title: E — Reading Skills for the AI Age
blocks.outcome: Students know both traditional and AI-assisted ways to understand an unfamiliar tool.

blocks.title: F — Wrap
blocks.outcome: Students know what's next and what to do before Session 2.

blocks.title: A — Recap + Markdown + Idea Brainstorm
blocks.outcome: Students understand markdown basics and have started thinking about project ideas.

blocks.title: B — Terminal Essentials
blocks.outcome: Students can navigate the filesystem and create/move files using core commands, and know to ask Claude for complex ones.

blocks.title: C — Git Setup + Basics
blocks.outcome: Students have git configured, understand the core workflow, and can use Claude for commit messages.

blocks.title: D — Editor & Workflow Choice
blocks.outcome: Students understand editor options and know editor choice isn't permanent.

blocks.title: E — Hands-on: First Contributions
blocks.outcome: Students have a personal repo and have opened a PR to the Tour Repo.

blocks.title: F — Wrap
blocks.outcome: Students know homework and feel a sense of progress.

blocks.title: A — Recap + Install
blocks.outcome: Every student has claude --version working by end of block.

blocks.title: B — Mental Model + Live Tool Exploration
blocks.outcome: Students can name the four concepts (Session, Context, Permission, Cost) and have seen /help, /usage, /clear live.

blocks.title: C — First Real Session
blocks.outcome: Students have watched a complete Claude Code session -- file creation, edit approval/rejection/undo, running commands.

blocks.title: D — Permission Modes + Planning Mode
blocks.outcome: Students understand the 4 primary permission modes, can switch between them, and know when to use each.

blocks.title: E — CLAUDE.md
blocks.outcome: Students have a CLAUDE.md in their project generated by /init and edited with at least one meaningful addition.

blocks.title: F — Cost & Token Awareness
blocks.outcome: Students can check their session cost with /usage and have realistic expectations for small vs large projects.

blocks.title: A — Recap + Common Issues
blocks.outcome: Students surface and resolve overnight issues; reconnect with session cost context.

blocks.title: B — Vague vs Specific Prompting
blocks.outcome: Students can write a specific prompt and articulate why specific prompts produce better results -- plus when vague is actually better.

blocks.title: C — Workflow Patterns
blocks.outcome: Students have tried at least one workflow pattern (Build, Refactor, Explain, Fix) on a real codebase.

blocks.title: D — AI Struggle Signals + Session Management
blocks.outcome: Students can identify the 4 struggle signals and apply the right response strategy (re-prompt, manual intervention, /clear, /compact).

blocks.title: E — Buddy Pair Exercise
blocks.outcome: Students have experienced prompting Claude Code collaboratively -- one typing, one directing -- and have a mini-feature to show.

blocks.title: F — Wrap
blocks.outcome: Students know homework expectations, understand the idea submission deadline, and leave feeling capable.

blocks.title: A — Recap + Homework Check
blocks.outcome: Chapter 2 work acknowledged, ideas visible, students oriented for the team announcement.

blocks.title: B — Team Announcement
blocks.outcome: 3 teams of 3 formed, rationale understood, initial emotional reaction processed.

blocks.title: C — Roles Explained
blocks.outcome: Students understand the three roles, the rotation rule, and how each role relates to Claude.

blocks.title: D — Team Ice-breaker
blocks.outcome: Team members know each other's working styles, strengths, struggles, and have 2-3 team norms established.

blocks.title: E — Idea Evaluation Workshop
blocks.outcome: Each team has narrowed to 2-3 candidate ideas evaluated against a concrete framework.

blocks.title: F — Wrap + Pitch Prep
blocks.outcome: Teams have the pitch structure, understand the homework, and know what Session 2 brings.

blocks.title: A — Working Agreement Finalize
blocks.outcome: Each team has a personalized working agreement committed to their repo.

blocks.title: B — SDD Intro
blocks.outcome: Students understand the spectrum from vibe coding to spec-driven development and know why specs matter more in the AI era.

blocks.title: C — Team Pitches
blocks.outcome: All 3 teams have pitched, received feedback, and adjusted their project scope.

blocks.title: D — Architecture Decision
blocks.outcome: Each team has a documented stack decision (frontend, backend, database, deployment) with rationale.

blocks.title: E — Repo Setup + First Team Commit
blocks.outcome: Each team has a GitHub repo with README, CLAUDE.md, .gitignore, and working agreement — all 3 members can push and pull.

blocks.title: F — Week 1 Role Assignment
blocks.outcome: Each team has Anchor/Builder/Driver assigned for Chapter 4, with a clear understanding of what each role does next week.

blocks.title: A — Problem Statement
blocks.outcome: Students can name the context problem, spot its symptoms, and map the four solution categories.

blocks.title: B — MCP Basics + Install Methods
blocks.outcome: Students understand MCP and can install servers through both methods.

blocks.title: C — Context7 Demo
blocks.outcome: Students have seen Context7 fix library-doc hallucinations in real time.

blocks.title: D — claude-mem Demo
blocks.outcome: Students have seen claude-mem persist memory across sessions.

blocks.title: E — LLM Wiki Pattern
blocks.outcome: Students can start an LLM Wiki immediately, with zero tooling.

blocks.title: F — Try What's Interesting
blocks.outcome: Students have picked something to explore and started trying it.

blocks.title: A — Recap + Galleries Tour
blocks.outcome: Students can navigate MCP galleries and use Claude to discover tools.

blocks.title: B — Custom MCP Concept
blocks.outcome: Students understand how an MCP server is built — and see it's within reach.

blocks.title: C — Team Build Session
blocks.outcome: Each team has wired at least one context tool into their workflow and made real build progress.

blocks.title: D — Share-out + Reflection
blocks.outcome: Teams share what worked and learn from each other's context-tool experience.

blocks.title: E — Wrap + Homework
blocks.outcome: Students know what's due and what's next.

blocks.title: A — Recap + Skills Intro + Install
blocks.outcome: Students understand what Skills are, how they differ from MCP, and have installed at least one Skill from the gallery.

blocks.title: B — Skills in Practice
blocks.outcome: Students understand Skill anatomy (SKILL.md + YAML frontmatter + resources), know when to build custom Skills, and have installed one from a gallery.

blocks.title: C — Subagents
blocks.outcome: Students understand the subagent concept, can distinguish Skills from subagents, and have seen one in action.

blocks.title: D — Multi-agent Orchestration
blocks.outcome: Students are aware that multi-agent orchestration exists, understand the concept, and know where to explore further. Not expected to implement.

blocks.title: E — Workflow Methodology Tour
blocks.outcome: Students are aware of three structured workflow methodologies, have seen each demoed, and can choose one to explore further.

blocks.title: F — Wrap
blocks.outcome: Students know what's coming in Session 2 and have homework direction.

blocks.title: A — Claude Code Structure
blocks.outcome: Students understand the Claude Code file hierarchy, know where every configuration lives, and can navigate settings confidently.

blocks.title: B — Capability Tour: Browser + Programmatic
blocks.outcome: Students know Claude can control browsers (Playwright MCP), know the Claude API/SDK exists for programmatic access, and understand the "many surfaces" framing.

blocks.title: C — Hooks Proper Coverage
blocks.outcome: Students understand hooks as lifecycle events, have seen a working hook, and know common use cases.

blocks.title: D — Token Optimization
blocks.outcome: Students understand token costs matter, know /compact and at least one optimization tool, and will experiment in homework.

blocks.title: E — Mid-point Review
blocks.outcome: Every team has demoed current progress, received feedback, and has a clear path for the remaining chapters.

blocks.title: F — Wrap
blocks.outcome: Students know homework, Chapter 6 preview, and role rotation plan.

blocks.title: A — Polish Philosophy + Claude Review
blocks.outcome: Students understand the polish-first-deploy-second philosophy and have seen Claude review their own project.

blocks.title: B — Claude-driven UX Polish
blocks.outcome: Teams have implemented at least 2-3 UX improvements via Claude, with the instructor validating rather than directing.

blocks.title: C — Claude-driven README
blocks.outcome: Every team has a comprehensive, professional README.

blocks.title: D — Analytics
blocks.outcome: Teams understand analytics basics and have a tracking setup ready for post-deploy.

blocks.title: E — Claude-driven Demo Prep
blocks.outcome: Every team has a 5-minute demo script draft, structured and practiced once.

blocks.title: F — Wrap
blocks.outcome: Teams share progress, know pre-deploy homework, and have platform accounts ready.

blocks.title: A — Deploy Options + Claude Analysis
blocks.outcome: Each team has chosen a deployment platform with reasoning.

blocks.title: B — Claude-driven Deploy
blocks.outcome: Every team has a live URL.

blocks.title: C — AI-Augmented Security
blocks.outcome: Teams have audited their project's security using AI, understand .env workflow, and are aware of prompt injection.

blocks.title: D — Custom Domain
blocks.outcome: Students understand domains and DNS basics. Teams with domains can configure them; others know how for later.

blocks.title: E — Live URL Share + Wrap
blocks.outcome: Every team has shared their live URL. Channel has all project links. Momentum into Chapter 7.

blocks.title: A — Recap + Landscape Framing
blocks.outcome: Students understand why this chapter breaks from the Claude Code focus, and feel excited rather than overwhelmed.

blocks.title: B — Cursor vs Claude Code
blocks.outcome: Students understand the IDE-first vs CLI-first distinction and can articulate when each approach fits.

blocks.title: C — UI-First Generation Tour
blocks.outcome: Students know what UI-first generation tools are, when they're useful, and how they complement Claude Code.

blocks.title: D — GitHub Copilot
blocks.outcome: Students understand inline completion as a category and how Copilot fits alongside Claude Code.

blocks.title: E — Chat — Still Relevant?
blocks.outcome: Students can articulate when chat-based AI is the right tool instead of Claude Code.

blocks.title: F — Comparison Matrix + Discovery Skill
blocks.outcome: Students have a comparison framework they built themselves, and a repeatable method for discovering new tools.

blocks.title: G — Wrap
blocks.outcome: Students know what to prepare for Session 2 and feel energized, not overwhelmed.

blocks.title: A — Recap + Sprint Brief
blocks.outcome: Teams know their sprint priorities and the session structure.

blocks.title: B — Build Sprint Block 1
blocks.outcome: Each team has made meaningful progress toward demo-readiness, with instructor guidance.

blocks.title: C — Buddy Team Showcase
blocks.outcome: Each team has presented their project to peers, received structured feedback, and rehearsed for Demo Day.

blocks.title: E — Wrap + Demo Day Brief
blocks.outcome: Students know exactly what to prepare for Demo Day and feel ready.

blocks.title: A — Opening + Format Brief
blocks.outcome: Everyone understands the format, the spirit, and the ground rules. Nerves are settled.

blocks.title: B — Team A Demo
blocks.outcome: Team A presents their project and honestly names AI's role.

blocks.title: C — Team B Demo
blocks.outcome: Team B presents. Same structure.

blocks.title: D — Team C Demo
blocks.outcome: Team C presents. All teams have now demoed.

blocks.title: E — Break
blocks.outcome: Energy reset. Informal connection.

blocks.title: F — Group Reflection
blocks.outcome: Shared understanding of wins, failures, and how AI felt as a partner.

blocks.title: G — Photo + Energy Transfer
blocks.outcome: Group photo captured. Tour Repo gets its final commit from each team.

blocks.title: A — Retrospective
blocks.outcome: Honest feedback collected. Identity check confirms the Tour stayed true to its philosophy.

blocks.title: B — Continued Learning Paths
blocks.outcome: Students have a clear, non-overwhelming map of what to explore after the Tour.

blocks.title: C — Community Formation
blocks.outcome: Alumni channel established. Co-working sessions announced. Project continuation encouraged.

blocks.title: D — Next Cohort
blocks.outcome: Students know the Tour's future. Loose alumni engagement seeds planted.

blocks.title: E — Open Q&A
blocks.outcome: Remaining questions answered. Students feel heard.

blocks.title: F — Send-off + Final Words
blocks.outcome: Every student speaks. The Tour closes with warmth, honesty, and forward energy.
